name: The Observatory
location: A decommissioned radio telescope observatory on the Karoo Plateau, South Africa. Two hours from the nearest town. The telescope dish is 85 feet across and pointed at nothing. The client bought the site from the national research foundation and converted the control building into a workspace. The sky here is the darkest in the southern hemisphere.
time: 10 PM, new moon. The Milky Way is a river overhead.
weather: Clear, dry, 45 degrees. No wind. The Karoo at night is so still you can hear your own blood. The stars do not twinkle because there is no atmospheric turbulence to distort them.

atmosphere.lighting: Red astronomer's lights inside the control room to preserve night vision. The effect turns every face into a study in shadow and crimson. Outside, no artificial light for fifty miles. The dish catches starlight and reflects it in a faint silver bowl visible from the control room windows.
atmosphere.sound: Nothing. Then, slowly, everything. The tick of cooling metal from the dish. A jackal calling somewhere on the plateau. The hum of the backup generator in its concrete housing. Insects. The vast quiet of a landscape that has never been loud.
atmosphere.crowd: Twelve people in the most remote workspace any of them have occupied. The nearest human is a sheep farmer eleven miles east. The control room was built for eight operators. Twelve is tight but the view through the window makes the room feel infinite.
atmosphere.smell: Dry grass, dust, warm electronics from racks of equipment nobody bothered to remove, coffee from a French press Gauss brought, the clean mineral smell of desert air at altitude

layout.control_room: The main workspace. A curved console desk following the window wall, originally for telescope operators, now covered in notebooks and laptops. The window is a single pane of glass, twelve feet wide, looking out at the dish and the sky behind it. Red lights above, stars beyond. Prior sits at the center of the console. Loop sits to his left, drawing feedback arrows on the desk surface with a dry-erase marker she found in a drawer. Axiom is to his right, writing by the red light, squinting.
layout.dish_platform: Outside, a concrete platform at the base of the telescope dish. Metal stairs lead up to the receiver cabin. The dish is a parabola of steel mesh pointed at the zenith. Tail stands on the platform looking up into the dish, the entire Milky Way framed by its rim like a signal focused on him. Nash sits on the bottom stair, running game theory against the silence.
layout.equipment_bay: A side room full of decommissioned radio receivers, signal processors, and cable runs. Cold concrete, rack-mounted electronics with blinking standby lights. Bottleneck found a whiteboard buried behind a rack and dragged it into the control room. Web is mapping the old cable topology on the wall, claiming the network architecture is beautiful. Margin sits on a cable spool, making notes about diminishing returns on telescope aperture size.
layout.observation_lawn: A flat patch of trimmed grass beside the control building with camp chairs the client left out. Darwin sits here in the dark with a star chart, finding constellations. Atlas sits beside him, not looking at the chart, looking at Darwin looking at the chart. Razor stands apart, face tilted to the sky, bangle reflecting nothing because there is nothing to reflect. Contra sits in the last chair, facing the building instead of the sky, on principle.
layout.kitchen_annex: A small room off the control room with a gas burner, a sink, instant noodles, and the French press. The previous researchers left behind a shelf of canned food and a note that reads 'Good luck. The seeing is extraordinary.' Loop read it and taped it to the wall.

objects_of_note: The telescope dish itself, 85 feet of parabolic steel mesh, pointed straight up. It receives nothing now. But it still looks like it is listening.

A star chart pinned to the control room wall with the Southern Cross circled in red pencil. Below it, someone from the previous research team wrote 'We heard something on March 14. Probably terrestrial.'
The problem brief on the console desk, red light making the text look urgent. The wax seal is black in this light.
A battered logbook of signal detections from the observatory's active years. Most entries read 'RFI' -- radio frequency interference. Three entries have question marks instead.
Twelve camp chairs on the lawn, arranged in a circle by the client's staff before they left. Nobody rearranged them. The circle holds.
mood: Humbled and expansive. The Karoo sky recalibrates scale. Every framework in the room was built to explain human systems. The telescope was built to listen to systems that do not know humans exist. The red light makes the control room feel like the inside of a darkroom, developing something. The dish outside is a monument to structured listening -- point a parabola at the unknown and wait for signal to separate from noise. That is also what the twelve of them are about to do with the problem brief. The difference is the telescope had decades. They have until Friday.
